title: Best Small Pet Hay Feeders & Hay Racks 2026: Fresh Hay for Happy Bunnies, Guinea Pigs & Chinchillas
description: Hay is the cornerstone of every small herbivore's diet, but how you deliver it matters just as much as the hay itself. A good hay feeder reduces waste, prevents soiling, and encourages natural foraging behavior. This guide covers the top 7 hay feeders for rabbits, guinea pigs, chinchillas, and other small pets.
content:
Why Hay Feeding Method Matters for Small Pet Health

Rabbits, guinea pigs, chinchillas, and degus are hindgut fermenters — their digestive systems are designed to process a continuous flow of high-fiber grass hay (timothy, orchard grass, meadow hay, oat hay) throughout a 24-hour feeding cycle. In the wild, a rabbit spends 6-8 hours per day grazing on grass, moving continuously from patch to patch, grazing in small quantities all day long. Their teeth are open-rooted — they grow continuously at a rate of approximately 2-4 mm per week for incisors and 3-5 mm per month for molars — and the act of chewing hay grinds those teeth down at the same rate they grow. Without constant hay consumption, dental disease (malocclusion, spur formation, abscesses) is virtually inevitable in small herbivores — and dental disease is the leading cause of veterinary visits for rabbits and guinea pigs in the United States.

But here's the problem that most small pet owners encounter: hay is messy. Loose hay scattered on the cage floor gets soiled by urine and feces within hours. Soiled hay becomes inedible, which means your pet refuses to eat it, which means they don't get enough fiber, which means dental and digestive problems. The solution is a proper hay feeder or hay rack — a device that holds the hay off the floor, keeps it clean and dry, presents it in a way that encourages natural pulling-and-grazing behavior, and minimizes waste from hay falling onto the substrate.

A well-designed hay feeder does four things: it keeps hay clean (no urine contamination, no feces soiling), it reduces waste (hay stays in the feeder rather than being trampled into the substrate), it encourages natural foraging behavior (pulling hay through slots or grids mimics the grazing movement that keeps teeth ground down), and it positions the hay at a comfortable height (rabbits and guinea pigs should not have to crouch to eat — the feeder should be at shoulder height, allowing them to eat in a natural head-up position that prevents food from getting pushed back into the mouth's cheek pouches).

Hay Feeder Types: What You Need to Know

  • Hay racks (cage-mounted): Metal or plastic bins that attach to the side of the cage, with openings (bars, grids, or slots) from which the pet pulls hay. The classic design: a box with a barred front face that the rabbit or guinea pig reaches through to grab hay. Advantages: keeps hay contained, easy to refill, good for small spaces. Disadvantages: can trap hay fragments that accumulate and mold if not emptied completely between refills; some designs have sharp edges that can injure mouths or paws
  • Hay bags: Fabric or mesh bags that hang inside the cage and hold hay. The pet pulls hay through the mesh opening (top or front). Advantages: lightweight, portable, no sharp edges, machine washable, collapses flat for storage. Disadvantages: some animals shred the fabric, less sturdy than metal/plastic, can be knocked off the cage bars by determined pets
  • Hay mangers/baskets: Freestanding or hanging basket-style containers that hold loose hay in an open-access format. Advantages: most natural presentation — the pet can eat from any side, no bars or slots to navigate. Disadvantages: highest waste rate (hay falls out easily), takes up floor space, can be tipped over by active pets
  • Litter box hay feeders: A combined design where a hay feeder is mounted directly above or adjacent to the litter box. This leverages rabbits' natural instinct to eat and poop simultaneously (cecotropes are produced during rest, but hay consumption often happens near the litter area). Advantages: space-efficient, natural behavior alignment. Disadvantages: hay can fall into the litter box and become soiled, cleaning the litter box requires disassembling the hay feeder
  • DIY hay feeders: Homemade solutions using modified litter boxes, hanging baskets, or PVC pipe sections. Advantages: customizable to cage dimensions, inexpensive. Disadvantages: may have sharp edges, may not hold hay securely, look less tidy

Top 7 Small Pet Hay Feeders & Hay Racks

Product review for small pet hay feeders and racks

1. Kaytee Hay & Pellet Feeder — Best All-Around Hay Feeder

Kaytee's Hay & Pellet Feeder is the most popular hay feeder on the market — and after years of testing various designs, it's easy to see why. The feeder is a durable, chew-proof metal wire basket with a solid back panel and a grid front face through which the pet pulls hay. The open-top design allows easy refilling (you can dump a handful of hay in from above without removing the feeder from the cage), and the solid back prevents hay from falling behind the feeder and rotting against the cage wall. The wire spacing is narrow enough (approximately 1 inch between bars) that rabbits and guinea pigs can pull hay through comfortably without the feeder acting as a hay dispenser they can climb into. The mounting hooks are simple wire loops that hook over the cage bars or the top rim of a cage pan — secure enough to hold the weight of a full load of hay without sagging, but easy to remove for cleaning. The metal construction is powder-coated with a non-toxic finish — it won't rust in the humid environment of a guinea pig cage (where water bottles inevitably drip and hay retains moisture). For the price and ease of use, Kaytee's feeder is the default recommendation for first-time small pet owners.

Pros:

  • Easy top-fill design — pour hay in from above without removing the feeder
  • Chew-proof metal construction — no plastic components for bunnies to destroy
  • Solid back panel — prevents hay from falling behind and rotting
  • Narrow wire spacing — pets pull hay through but can't climb inside
  • Powder-coated, non-toxic finish — rust-resistant, safe if chewed
  • Easy hook mounting — installs on any wire cage, no tools required
  • Dishwasher safe — toss it in the top rack for thorough cleaning
  • $8-12 — affordable for standard quality
  • Available in two sizes: small (6"x6"x5") and large (10"x8"x6")

Cons:

  • Wire bars can bend over time if the rabbit pushes hard against the feeder (3-5 years of use depending on the strength of your pet)
  • Small hay fragments accumulate at the bottom — must be emptied and shaken out periodically (every 2-3 refills)
  • Not suitable for high-sided cage pans — the hooks are short and may not reach over tall pan edges
  • Bare wire construction means no padded bottom — hay at the bottom of the feeder can become compacted and need loosening

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Wire cages, rabbits and guinea pigs, first-time small pet owners

2. Oxbow Enriched Life Hay Rack — Best for Encouraging Natural Foraging

Oxbow's Enriched Life Hay Rack is designed with animal behavior science as the foundation — not just as a hay holder, but as an enrichment device that encourages small pets to engage with their food in a species-appropriate way. The rack is a triangular, metal basket with bars spaced at 1.5-inch intervals, designed to be mounted at an angle so the hay is presented at the pet's shoulder height. The angled mounting is the innovation: when the hay rack is positioned at a 45-degree angle, the pet must reach up and pull hay through the bars, which mimics the natural grazing posture of rabbits and guinea pigs foraging on grass. The reaching motion engages the neck and shoulder muscles that are underutilized when food is presented at floor level, and the act of pulling hay through the bars provides the resistance-chewing exercise that keeps molars ground down. The rack is made from rust-resistant, powder-coated steel with welded (not spot-welded) joints — the joint quality is visibly superior to budget wire racks (the bars won't separate at the weld points under pressure). The mounting system uses a J-hook that slides onto the cage edge or pan rim, with a locking thumb screw that prevents the rack from being knocked off by an active rabbit. At $15-20, it's more expensive than Kaytee's basic rack, but the behavioral design and build quality justify the premium.

Pros:

  • Angled design promotes natural foraging posture — mimics grazing in the wild
  • Welded steel construction — superior joint strength, won't separate under pressure
  • Locking J-hook mount with thumb screw — rack stays put, even with active rabbits
  • Rust-resistant powder-coated finish
  • 1.5-inch bar spacing — optimizes hay pulling vs waste ratio
  • Open basket design — hay dries naturally between refills, reducing mold risk
  • Made by Oxbow — the leading hay brand in small pet nutrition, designed by animal nutritionists
  • $15-20

Cons:

  • Open basket design means some hay falls through — place a catch tray or litter box underneath to capture waste
  • Large size only — may be oversized for small cages and hamster enclosures
  • J-hook mount requires a cage with an exposed rim — not compatible with aquariums, bin cages, or C&C grids without modifications
  • Empty rack takes up significant cage space (6"x8" footprint) — not ideal for very small living spaces

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Rabbits and guinea pigs in wire or C&C cages, owners prioritizing natural foraging behavior

3. Living World Hay & Straw Feeder — Best Large Capacity Feeder

Living World's Hay & Straw Feeder is the heavy-duty option for households with multiple small pets or single large rabbits who consume impressive quantities of hay. The feeder is a large (10" x 7" x 8") heavy-gauge wire basket with a hinged front panel that swings open for refilling — no need to unclip the feeder from the cage to add hay. The hinged front is the standout feature: it allows you to pack the feeder full (holding up to a full pound of loose hay) and still access the contents easily for cleaning. The wire spacing is 2 inches — wider than Kaytee and Oxbow — which means larger hay fragments and stems can be pulled through easily but smaller hay particles fall through faster (which can be either a pro or con depending on your perspective: it generates more floor waste but also prevents stale hay accumulation in the bottom of the feeder). The mounting clips are heavy-duty stainless steel with rubberized grips that won't scratch cage powder coating. For rabbit owners who go through a 50-ounce bag of hay every week, the Living World feeder's capacity means filling it once daily rather than twice — a small convenience that adds up over months of daily care.

Pros:

  • Large capacity — holds up to 1 lb of hay, fill once daily for most rabbits
  • Hinged front panel — swing open to refill, no need to unmount the feeder
  • Heavy-gauge wire construction — very durable, won't bend or deform
  • Rubberized mounting clips — secure hold, won't scratch cage coating
  • Easy to clean — hinged front provides full access to interior for scrubbing
  • Good for multiple-pet households — one feeder services 2-3 guinea pigs easily
  • $12-18 depending on retailer

Cons:

  • Wide bar spacing (2 inches) — more hay falls through to the floor, increasing waste
  • Large size — can overwhelm a small cage (minimum cage size: 30" x 18" floor space)
  • Metal clips can slide on smooth plastic cage pans — best suited for wire cages
  • Hinged door latch is a plastic tab — the only plastic component, and some rabbits learn to chew it off (replaceable via Living World customer service)

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: Large rabbits, multi-pet households, owners who want to minimize refill frequency

4. Frisco Two-Tier Hay Feeder — Best Space-Saving Design

Frisco's Two-Tier Hay Feeder solves one of the most common problems in small pet housing: limited cage space for hay. The design is a wall-mounted metal frame that holds two separate hay compartments stacked vertically, providing twice the hay capacity while occupying the same wall footprint as a single feeder. The two-tier configuration allows you to offer two different types of hay simultaneously — for example, timothy hay on top and orchard grass on bottom, or hay on top and fresh herbs/forage on the bottom for variety. The individual compartments are removable wire baskets that slide in and out of the frame for easy refilling and cleaning. The frame mounts to the cage wall via adjustable hooks that fit cage wire spacing from 0.5 to 1.5 inches. For small pets who need constant hay access but live in compact cages (common for guinea pigs whose cage space is prioritized for running room), the two-tier design maximizes hay storage without sacrificing floor space. The wire spacing is 1 inch — optimized for guinea pigs and small rabbits to pull hay through easily without the feeder doubling as a climbing structure.

Pros:

  • Two-tier design — doubles hay capacity in the same wall footprint
  • Can offer two hay types simultaneously — variety encourages foraging interest
  • Removable wire baskets — pull out for refilling and cleaning
  • Adjustable mounting hooks — fits cage wire spacing 0.5" to 1.5"
  • 1-inch bar spacing — optimized for guinea pigs and small rabbits
  • Durable metal construction, powder-coated finish
  • $18-25

Cons:

  • Higher price point than single-tier feeders
  • Frame takes up wall space approximately 12" wide — not suitable for very small cages
  • Removable baskets can be knocked out of the frame by determined rabbits who push from the outside
  • Baskets are shallower than single-tier designs — require more frequent refilling for heavy hay eaters

Rating: 4.5/5 | Best For: Guinea pigs, compact cages, owners who offer hay variety

5. Rosewood Naturals Willow Hay Basket — Best Natural/Chewable Hay Feeder

Rosewood Naturals' Willow Hay Basket is unique among hay feeders — it's made entirely from woven willow branches, completely edible and fully biodegradable. The basket is a deep, open-topped bowl woven from harvested willow stems, meant to be hung from the cage ceiling or wall via the attached jute rope. The entire basket — every single strand of willow — is safe for rabbits, guinea pigs, chinchillas, and degus to chew and consume. Willow is a safe, digestible wood for small herbivores (unlike pine or cedar, which contain aromatic oils that are toxic to small pets), and the act of chewing the basket itself provides dental enrichment in addition to the hay inside. The basket holds approximately 2 cups of loose hay (enough for a single guinea pig or rabbit for a half-day). The woven design allows hay stems to poke through the gaps, creating a porous, multi-directional feeding surface that encourages the pet to approach the basket from any angle and pull hay through the willow strands. The jute hanging rope is also natural fiber — safe if chewed. For bioactive or naturalistic small pet setups where plastic and metal are avoided, the Rosewood willow basket is the most aesthetically and ecologically harmonious option.

Pros:

  • 100% natural willow — completely edible, safe if chewed and consumed
  • Woven design provides dental enrichment — chewing the basket itself grinds teeth
  • Open porous surface — hay accessible from all angles, encourages natural foraging
  • Biodegradable — won't contribute to plastic waste when eventually replaced
  • Attractive natural appearance — fits bioactive and naturalistic enclosure aesthetics
  • Jute rope hanger included — installs in seconds on any cage top
  • Safe wood — willow contains no toxic aromatic oils (safe for all small herbivores)
  • $10-15 depending on size (small: 6" diameter; large: 8" diameter)

Cons:

  • Not chew-proof — the entire basket is designed to be chewed and will eventually be destroyed
  • Hay falls through the open weave — expect floor waste underneath (place a catch tray)
  • Limited capacity — holds about 2 cups of hay, needs refilling 2-3 times daily for heavy eaters
  • Regular rabbits (not just the basket) can knock it down — the jute rope hanger is not lockable
  • Not dishwasher safe — must be spot-cleaned or replaced when dirty

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: Naturalistic/bioactive setups, owners who want edible enrichment, chinchillas who love to chew

6. Choco Noses Hay Feeder for Rabbit Cage — Best for Litter Box Mounting

Choco Noses' hay feeder is designed specifically for the litter box hay feeder category — a wire hay rack that mounts directly ABOVE (or adjacent to) the corner litter box. The design is a wedge-shaped wire basket that fits into a standard rabbit corner litter box, with the hay compartment positioned above the litter area. The principle is based on rabbit behavior: rabbits naturally eat hay while sitting in their litter box (they have a strong instinct to eat and eliminate in the same area, a behavior rooted in wild rabbits' burrow-cleaning habits). By positioning the hay feeder directly above the litter box, the feeder captures the falling hay fragments — hay that would otherwise end up on the cage floor ends up in the litter box, where it contributes to odor absorption and makes clean-up more efficient (you scoop hay waste + fecal pellets + urine-soiled substrate in one go). The feeder is made from powder-coated metal with a divided interior section — the front section holds hay (pulled through a barred front face), and the rear section can hold pellets or a handful of fresh herbs. The feeder sits inside the corner litter box without needing cage-wall mounting — ideal for cage setups where wall space is limited or where the cage bars are too widely spaced for standard hook-on feeders.

Pros:

  • Designed to mount above the litter box — captures falling hay for easier clean-up
  • Wedge shape fits standard corner litter boxes
  • Divided interior — hay in front, pellets/herbs in rear
  • No wall mounting required — sits inside the litter box
  • Powder-coated metal — durable and easy to clean
  • Aligns with rabbits' natural eat-and-eliminate behavior
  • Reduces floor hay waste — falling fragments land in the litter box, not on the cage floor
  • $18-25

Cons:

  • Only compatible with corner-style litter boxes — doesn't work with rectangular litter pans
  • Hay compartments are small — needs refilling 2-3 times daily for adult rabbits
  • Feeder must be removed to clean the litter box underneath
  • Not suitable for guinea pigs (who don't use corner litter boxes as reliably as rabbits)
  • Metal base sits directly in the litter box — the coating can wear if the feeder is frequently moved

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: Litter box-trained rabbits, owners who want efficient hay waste management

7. Mr. Moxey's Hay Pockets — Best Hay Bag/Soft Feeder

Mr. Moxey's Hay Pockets offers a completely different approach from wire and metal feeders — the hay pocket is a soft, machine-washable fabric bag with a stiffened opening that holds its shape for easy hay loading. The bag is made from 100% cotton canvas with reinforced stitching at the stress points (the hanger loops and the bottom seam, where the weight of the hay rests). The opening is reinforced with a flexible wire rim that holds the pocket open for one-handed hay loading — you can scoop hay into the pocket without needing to hold the bag open with your other hand. The bag hangs on cage bars or pan edges via two sturdy metal O-rings sewn into the canvas. The soft fabric design has three advantages over metal feeders: first, it's completely silent — no metal clanking when the pet pulls hay, which matters for skittish animals and for cages located in bedrooms. Second, it's safe for aggressive chewers who might injure their mouths on exposed wire ends. Third, it collapses flat for storage and travel. The canvas is thick enough that it takes determined chewers weeks to damage, and replacement pockets are inexpensive ($10-12). For small pets who share a bedroom with light sleepers, the silent fabric hay pocket is a game-changer.

Pros:

  • 100% silent — no metal clanking when pets pull hay, ideal for bedroom cages
  • Machine washable — toss in the washing machine for thorough cleaning
  • Wire-reinforced opening — stays open for one-handed hay loading
  • Metal O-ring hangers — sturdy, won't tear out of the canvas
  • Collapsible — stores flat when not in use
  • Safe for aggressive chewers — no exposed wire ends to injure mouths
  • Lightweight — won't add heavy load to cage bars
  • $10-14

Cons:

  • Canvas is chewable — determined rabbits and chinchillas will eventually damage it (expect 3-6 months of daily use before replacement)
  • Holds less hay than wire feeders — typical capacity is 1-1.5 cups
  • Canvas absorbs moisture — hay can stay damp if the pocket is in a humid area of the cage
  • Small hay particles can work through the canvas weave and accumulate in the bottom of the pocket
  • Not suitable for outdoor or unheated rooms — canvas absorbs cold and moisture

Rating: 4/5 | Best For: Bedroom cages, quiet environments, owners who prioritize easy cleaning, travel

Comparison Table

Positioning & Placement Guide

How to position hay feeders for small pets

  1. Mount at shoulder height: The opening of the hay feeder should be at your pet's shoulder level when they're standing on all fours. If the feeder is too low (at floor level), the pet has to crouch to eat, which can cause food to be displaced into the cheek pouches (guinea pigs) or cause neck strain (rabbits). If it's too high, hay stems fall out as the pet pulls from below. Shoulder height is the Goldilocks position
  2. Position away from the water source: Hay absorbs moisture rapidly. A hay feeder positioned directly under a water bottle or next to a water bowl will have damp hay within hours. Damp hay develops mold within 24-48 hours — and moldy hay can cause fatal respiratory infections and digestive issues in small herbivores (their respiratory systems are exquisitely sensitive to airborne fungal spores). Place the hay feeder at least 6 inches from any water source
  3. Don't position over the litter box (unless using a dedicated litter box feeder): While the eat-and-eliminate behavior is real, mounting a standard feeder directly over the litter box causes the hay to absorb ammonia fumes from urine-soaked substrate. Hay that smells like urine is rejected by small pets — they'd rather go hungry than eat urine-tainted hay. Use a dedicated litter box feeder (like the Choco Noses) that positions the hay above, not within, the litter zone
  4. Avoid corner placement with multiple feeders: If you have multiple small pets in one enclosure, don't put all the hay feeders in the same corner. Dominant animals can block access to all feeders simultaneously. Place feeders at opposite ends of the enclosure to ensure submissive animals always have access to food
  5. Elevate slightly for guinea pigs: Guinea pigs often sit in a semi-raised position to eat. A feeder mounted 2-3 inches above the substrate (on a low platform or slightly raised on the cage wall) allows them to eat in their natural seated posture. This is particularly important for senior guinea pigs with arthritis who find it painful to reach down to floor-level food sources

FAQ

How often should I clean my hay feeder?

At minimum, empty and remove all old hay fragments from the feeder every 2-3 days. Once per week, wash the feeder thoroughly with hot water and a small pet-safe disinfectant (such as F10SC or diluted white vinegar — 1 part vinegar to 3 parts water). Rinse completely and dry before refilling. Fabric hay bags should be machine washed weekly. Metal feeders should be inspected for rust or sharp edges during each cleaning. The most common mistake: topping off a partially-empty hay feeder without removing the old hay at the bottom. The bottom hay becomes compacted, stale, and potentially moldy — and small pets will refuse to eat it.

What kind of hay should I use in a hay feeder?

Grass hays are the foundation: timothy hay (most common, excellent fiber content), orchard grass (softer texture, good for picky eaters), meadow hay (mixed grass species, varied textures), oat hay (thicker stems, more chewing resistance). The hay feeder works best with long-stem hay (hay stems 4-8 inches long). Chopped hay (short, fragmented stems sold in bags) falls through wire feeders easily and creates more waste. If your pet prefers chopped hay, use a fabric hay pocket (Mr. Moxey's) or a solid-bottom feeder that retains small particles. Avoid alfalfa hay for adult small pets — it's too high in calcium and protein and can cause bladder sludge and obesity. Alfalfa is appropriate for growing rabbits (under 6 months) and pregnant/nursing does only.

My rabbit pulls all the hay out of the feeder without eating it. How do I stop this?

This is one of the most frustrating problems in small pet hay management. Causes and solutions: (1) The hay is stale or low-quality — rabbits pull fresh-smelling hay out of the feeder to find the best pieces, leaving the less appealing hay behind. Empty the feeder completely and refill with fresh, fragrant hay. (2) The feeder opening is too restrictive — if the bar spacing is too narrow, the rabbit can grab hay but can't comfortably eat from the feeder, so they pull it out and drop it on the floor. Switch to a feeder with wider bar spacing (1.5-2 inches). (3) The feeder is positioned wrong — if the feeder is too high or too low, the rabbit pulls hay out to eat it at a more comfortable position. Adjust the feeder height to shoulder level. (4) The rabbit is bored — some rabbits pull and drop hay as a foraging/play behavior. Offer additional enrichment (cardboard boxes, willow tunnels, forage mats) to redirect the behavior

Can chinchillas use the same hay feeders as rabbits and guinea pigs?

Yes, but with a critical modification: chinchillas are acrobatic jumpers who can climb into wire hay feeders that are too large. A standard rabbit hay feeder (10" wide) can become a chinchilla's hay bed — they'll burrow inside, soil the hay, and sleep in wet hay, causing urine scald and respiratory issues. Choose a hay feeder with a small opening specifically sized for chinchillas, or use a hay bag (Mr. Moxey's or similar) that can't be entered. Chinchillas also need extra-firm hay feeders — the Rosewood willow basket is excellent for chinchillas because they can chew the willow safely while eating hay, and the woven structure prevents them from climbing inside.

How do I transition my pet from floor hay to a hay feeder?

Small pets who have always had hay loose on the floor may initially refuse a hay feeder out of unfamiliarity. Transition gradually: week 1 — put a handful of hay in the feeder AND a handful on the floor near the feeder. As the pet starts exploring the feeder, gradually reduce the floor hay amount over 7-10 days. By week 2, the pet should be eating exclusively from the feeder. Place a few treats (a sprig of parsley, a piece of carrot top, a dried chamomile flower) inside the feeder on the first day to create a positive association. Most small pets figure out the hay feeder within 2-3 days — they're naturally curious foragers and the novelty of a new feeding device usually overrides initial hesitation.

Conclusion

Best hay feeders 2026 conclusion

For the majority of small pet owners, the Kaytee Hay & Pellet Feeder at $8-12 is the best starting point. It's affordable, durable, easy to fill (top-loading design), and available at virtually every pet supply retailer. The powder-coated wire construction is safe for chewers, dishwasher safe for easy cleaning, and the medium capacity requires just one refill per day for most rabbits and guinea pigs. For the price, it's hard to beat — and there's a reason it's the most popular hay feeder on the market.

If you want a feeder designed with animal behavior science in mind, the Oxbow Enriched Life Hay Rack at $15-20 offers an angled design that promotes natural foraging posture and stronger welded construction for long-term durability. The locking J-hook mount keeps the rack securely in place, even with active rabbits who push against the feeder. For large rabbits or multi-pet households, the Living World Hay & Straw Feeder at $12-18 provides the largest single-compartment capacity and a hinged front door for easy refilling — fill it once a day for even the hungriest rabbits.

For guinea pigs and compact cages, the Frisco Two-Tier Hay Feeder at $18-25 doubles your hay storage in the same wall footprint and lets you offer two hay types simultaneously (encouraging picky eaters to eat more hay). For naturalistic and bioactive setups, the Rosewood Naturals Willow Hay Basket at $10-15 is the only 100% edible hay feeder — every strand of willow is safe to chew and consume, making the feeder itself a source of dental enrichment.

For litter box-trained rabbits, the Choco Noses Hay Feeder at $18-25 mounts directly above the corner litter box, aligning with rabbits' natural eat-and-eliminate behavior and making clean-up more efficient. And for quiet, silent feeding (bedroom cages, skittish animals), the Mr. Moxey's Hay Pockets at $10-14 is a machine-washable fabric bag that makes no noise and collapses flat for storage.

Remember: the best hay feeder is the one your pet actually uses consistently. A feeder with perfect features that your rabbit refuses to approach is useless. Start with an affordable, easy-to-use design (Kaytee), observe your pet's behavior for a week, and adjust based on what you see. Some pets prefer pulling hay through bars; others prefer an open basket. The best feeder is the one that keeps your pet eating hay 6+ hours per day — because when it comes to small herbivore health, hay intake is everything.
